turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- versus City of Louisville, et al. Mr. Frick, you may proceed with your argument.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: May it please the Court. I'd like to propose my remarks here with the idea that in a situation of this kind where you have a dispute between an individual and organized society, sympathy is always for the underdog, the individual. But there's a lot to be said for society, too. After all, organized society has the duty of preserving order. As a matter of fact, that was one of the purposes of constitutional government under the Constitution of the United States to ensure domestic tranquility. And of course, one of the ways of preserving order for all of its citizens is through these offenses and ordinances and statutes relating to loitering, disorderly conduct, vagrancy and minor offenses of that sort. They are designed to control society to -- to prevent individuals from congregating and as a result of idle minds stirring up crimes, which maybe of a serious nature and thereby injure society and all of the organized group as a whole. Now, with that theory in mind with which I am approaching this case, I'd like to get to this point. First of all, it seems to me that this case has been complicated by a certain number of irrelevancies, immaterialities and some speculation. And I'd like to enumerate what I consider some of those chief irrelevances. First of all, we have before this Court, a petition to review the proceedings of a single day, that is, the trial of February the 3rd, 1959, in the Louisville Police Court. The Court will have observed both from the briefs and from the argument that there's been a great deal to say about certain proceedings which happened in the Louisville Police Court on January the 20th. Now, they may have wondered, as did for some time when I first got into this case, how that happened to get into the record. In the proceedings of February the 3rd, an agreement was made at page 35 of the record to stipulate the testimony of Dr. Dean. That was page 34 of the record, I'm sorry, which was given at the trial of January the 20th. Now, when this stipulation was entered and it was agreed to by the prosecutor, Mr. Dougherty and by the attorney for the petitioner, it was then filed and since that testimony was included with the testimony of all the proceedings of January the 20th, the entire record was filed. Now, properly therefore, before this Court, there is not the proceedings of January the 20th as such, but only the stipulated testimony of Dr. Dean. So therefore, after we reach the first 35 pages in the record and the stipulated testimony of Dr. Dean, the remainder is surplusage. Now --

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Was there a contention made in the proceeding that we're reviewing that this was simply a course of conduct of harassment against this man?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir. That was to this extent, an avowal was tendered in which -- prepared by Mr. Lusky on behalf of his client that if he would be allowed to testify, he would testify as follows. That appears, I think --

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: That's that avow?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's that avow, yes, sir. That of course, was not allowed because in the feeling of the trial judge, that was irrelevant. That -- the issue he had before him was the offense which was charged on February the 3rd.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Where is the formal charge etcetera, on which we are here?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That appears on the first 35 pages of the record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, no, is there a formal charge?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir. It's an informal proceeding. In other words, the individual was arrested on February the 3rd.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It just came before the magistrate --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: It came before -- it came before the police court in due course --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) a speech may -- made these all. They're set forth what he claimed was the misconduct of the petitioner.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's -- that's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, where is that in terms? Where is -- where is the language? Does one have to be 35 pages to find out what he is charged with?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well [Laughs] the evidence -- there is no formal charge or language that appears --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Where did he --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- as such.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- where did he make a statement saying, “Your Honor, we are here because Sam Thompson did the following or misbehaved in the following manner.” Don't (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: As far as the police officer, that does not appear in the record. The --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You say --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- arresting slip --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You have to take you at your word. It doesn't appear in the record then I can't tell what he was charged with.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, is there any showing that he violated those charge, that he violated a particular section of the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, sir. That -- that is contained in the record. I am -- it's not included here. Perhaps, I should take risk. When an -- when an individual is arrested for a misdemeanor committed in the presence of the officer, he prepares that arrest slip which contains a charge which he has placed against him.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is that in the record?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That is not in this record, Your Honor

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Would be in the original record though?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Excuse me, sir?

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Would be in the original record, wouldn't it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir. I don't believe it's in the original record either, I don't see it in here. Of course, we didn't prepare this record, but --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is the original record here in the --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- with the clerk?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: It's not with the clerk. It's down in the office of the clerk of the Police Court in Louisville.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is it -- is it unfair to say that from what you've said without more, one could not find documented what it was that this petitioner really was charged with?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I hadn't thought of that point, but I -- I think you're right, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How can -- how can we -- how can we then determine whether the evidence supports the charge?If you don't know what the charge is, how can you tell whether the evidence supports it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, I know that he was charged with disorderly conduct and --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you may know, but [Laughs] --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: [Laughs] But I understand that perfectly, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: If you want --

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) I beg your pardon.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Excuse me.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Go ahead -- go ahead --

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: If you went to the records of the police court, would you find a piece of paper there that says --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- section so-and-so --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: TOM CLARK
turns.text: -- charged under section --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: TOM CLARK
turns.text: -- so-and-so, that's the -- that's the paper that's there in the police court?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's right. That is in the possession of the clerk of the police court.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: That in --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: It's just a small slip of paper with --

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Well, are the --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: It has the abbreviations for disorderly conduct. He put down, “D.C.” and for loitering, “Loit” and then they charge under this particular section of the ordinance.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: There is a piece of paper, although it's not here in this Court.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And no matter how informal the police court proceedings may be or how friendly, all the evidence before the police court judge would be -- would have to be confined within the scope of what that charge implied. Is that it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. In other words, that is a ground for frequent objection in police court that evidence is being produced which is not contained within the charge.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, if you -- if -- what is the maximum penalty that may be imposed?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: For loitering?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, we have it specified here. I don't recall offhand. I believe it's $100.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: $100, I believe.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: $100. Well, if it's decided against that it would -- $20, it could go up to your (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Certainly, if the sentence imposed is $20 or more, it may be appealed as a matter of right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: In other words, that (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: To the Circuit Court.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's right, to the Circuit Court.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are made then on -- on leave to go to the Court -- to your Court of Appeals?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: If it has a jurisdictional amount, it may then be appealed to the Court of Appeals.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How much is that, $100? What I want to know is could, could this kind of a case, if a -- if an appropriate fine is imposed, ever reach your Court of Appeals.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir, if the penalty was enough.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well then, if it comes before that Court of Appeals, they wouldn't have some documentation of what the charge is in order they can measure the evidence as against the charge. Is that right?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And we have -- but we're denied that opportunity here on this record, is that it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I don't think deny is exactly the right word. It's just a --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, we haven't got the opportunity.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I didn't even --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I mean --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) by the purposely can (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: We -- we're -- I'm not trying to keep anything from the Court.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I would like the Court to understand that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm sorry. If I conveyed such an impression, I didn't mean to. My language was impermissible.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: But I think both Mr. Lusky and I will be willing to stipulate that he was charged with loitering and disorderly conduct by the appropriate slip of paper in the Louisville Police Court.

turns.name: MR. ATTORNEY GENERAL
turns.text: If it please the Court, since counsel has invited a stipulation, I -- I would say that the original record which is in this Court does include the arrest slips, which items 1 and 2 in that record. I've asked the marshal to get the record and it will be here in a few minutes.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That was an error on my part. I did not know it was here.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How did the -- was there at any time, an attack or is the attack that that statute as such, without further ado, is violative of the Fourteenth Amendment? Are you going to -- is -- is that in the case, but you have to address yourself to that question or to the question that as applied, meaning by that that there was no evidence to sustain it, a judgment of -- a sentence was secured baseless so far as evidence is concerned? What is it you're going to speak against, Mr. Frick?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, I've had some difficulty --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- determining it myself, Your Honor. But as I understand it, one of the complaints made by the petitioner is that he -- his client was convicted without evidence and he had no method of either appeal or corrective judicial review. Now, (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But was there any evidence?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: In my opinion, there was, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: What was it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, I think we will recite what the evidence show. The evidence --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Excuse me, Mr. Frick. He directed himself now to both of the charges or the separate charges?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: They were separate charges, Your Honor, but they were tried together and the --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: The loitering?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: The loitering and the disorderly conduct were tried together and the conclusion of the testimony, the judge fixed a sentence of $10 on each offense.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Have you -- you defend both convictions?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: On the same evidence or on different --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: On the same evidence. There's a --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: The same.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- slight difference of evidence and of course, in all these cases for a police court where there are these minor offenses, not a great deal of evidence is adduced. But briefly, this is what happened. On this particular occasion, there were two police officers Officer Lacefield and Officer Barnett. They went into this tavern and barroom known as the Liberty Inn Café. Under the law of the City of Louisville, by ordinance, a condition is made before the issuance of any license for the sale of alcoholic beverages that it may be entered at any time by a police officer to observe whether or not orderly conduct is being carried on, on the premises. So, pursuant to that ordinance and as a routine check, these two individuals went into the Liberty Inn Café. When they arrived in the Liberty Inn Café, one officer testified, the only one who testified, they went up to the manager of the premises and asked him what that fellow was doing, pointing to Sam Thompson. And they asked him whether or not, he had bought anything to eat or drink while he had been there. The manager told them that he had bought nothing to eat, nor drink and that he had been there about 30 or 35 minutes. At this time, the officer testified, “Sam Thompson was standing in the center of the floor dancing by himself, neither eating nor drinking, but merely dancing.” And Officer Lacefield -- incidentally, Mr. Harlan, the officer who arrested the individual was Mr. Lacefield who did not know at the time of the arrest, anything about the previous trouble that Sam Thompson has been in. The other officer, Mr. Barnett, told the manager that he knew of some trouble that he had been in at the bus station before. Now, Officer Lacefield, in arresting this fellow, asked him what he was doing on the premises. Now, instead of saying that he was there to eat or drink anything, Mr. Thompson said that he was waiting for a bus. As the officer testified, the Liberty Inn Café faces on Liberty Street, which is a one-way street going west. If Mr. Thompson wishes to catch a bus, he must go out of that building, go down the street and down to the corner and catch a bus going east. So, on that basis, apparently, Officer Lacefield felt that there was not a satisfactory account given by Thompson and thereupon, arrested him.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Mr. Fink.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Mr. Fink.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Frick, sir.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Mr. Fink, may I ask you where you would have had him wait for the bus on this February 9th?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Of course, I am not going to tell anyone where he is to wait, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: At the bus station?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: But this is not at the bus station.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: And bear in mind (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: No, would you have him --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- five feet -- from five blocks or so from a bus station.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Would you have him wait at the bus station?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I think that he could.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: That's where he was arrested for doing that just three days before.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Some time before, Your Honor, but that is not the case which is before us now.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: There is another thing that he -- excuse me. Excuse me, sir?

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: He would go at gunshot at the bus station.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, that's what he [Laughter] -- that's what his attorney said, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Suppose --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now, here's what he could have done.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Suppose he'd said to the policeman, “It's none of your business why I'm here.” Would he -- would've -- would he have been within his constitutional rights? He might have been rude, but would he have been within his constitutional rights if he told him, “It's none of your business?”

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, I would say that under the circumstances, he would not have been within his constitutional rights.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Why, because he wasn't (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Because, under our ordinance --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Under -- under our ordinance, Your Honor --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Would that forbid a man from being in (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir. It does not forbid him there, but he should be there for the business purpose of the proprietor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, what's this --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: And he was not there on a business purpose of the proprietor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What is the police --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: He was there loitering.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- what is the policeman's business while a man is in a saloon? So long as he doesn't misbehave himself overtly.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well [Laughs] --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't understand --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: The -- the business is and under this loitering ordinance, an order to preserve peace, he realize that people loiter, that they will -- but idle minds think of mischief, which will cause damage to society.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Can you loiter inside of a barroom?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That is the point that we're --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Loitering means on the street, doesn't it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, now that gets to the other point. I would like to answer Mr. Whittaker's question first and then come to you. Insofar as where he could have waited, Your Honor, he could have waited at the bus stop itself, where he said he would -- intended to catch the bus which was --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) yes, but this was a cold night.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well [Laughs] a number of people have waited on cold nights, Your Honor, for a bus.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Is it a violation of the ordinance of Louisville for an old colored man to go into a bar and to be guilty of loitering and disorderly conduct simply because he taps his foot to music on a barroom floor as he waits for a bus on a cold winter night?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's very dramatic, Your Honor, but I plead what [Laughter] -- that what actually happened, not that he was tapping his foot. We have to bear this in mind, that where there is a conflict in evidence, Your Honor, and there has been a conviction, that conviction must be supported. Now, the officer testified --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Must be supported by what? By evidence.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Evidence, that's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: The -- the --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now, the evidence -- there was evidence that he was dancing, evidence of the arresting officer and ultimately, evidence of the manager of the barroom.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: What the man to the barroom say?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: On cross-examination, he was asked whether or not, the fellow was doing a shuffle dance and he answered, “Yes that he was.”

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That's precisely (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, he said, “You might call it shuffling,” but I would say he was tapping his feet to music. Now, isn't that what he said?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir, but I believe --

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: Is that --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- he also went further. I believe he admitted that it was a shuffle dance.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I don't know exactly where that is right here in the testimony.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: Is that a crime in Louisville?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: On page 27, but Mr. -- question, “But Mr. Thompson was dancing?” Answer, “He was shuffling around.” Question, “Doing a kind of shuffle dance?” Answer, “Yes.” That's the testimony of the manager of the --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: What is shuffle (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: What is --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Doing a shuffle dance. Now --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: What -- what is a shuffle dance?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Your Honor, I -- I presume it is [Laughter] some form of dancing which he uses a system of shuffling. Now, I was not present [Laughter] -- I was not present, of course and I can't demonstrate to the Court what was involved. Apparently --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is that illegal -- is that illegal in Louisville?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir. Shuffling is not illegal in Louisville. What is illegal, if it may please the Court, is dancing in the premises licensed to sell liquor. Now, that does not mean that the person who dances is committing a crime, but merely that the holder of the license is forbidden to permit dancing on his premises. In other words, a license holder is the one who violates that particular --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And we have got the license holder before us, have we? We have --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir, we do not.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- got a license --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: We do not have a license holder before us, but that demonstrates why this probably was not the type of business for which the operator of the tavern invited the public to attend on his premises. He certainly would not invite them to attend if, by so doing, he would jeopardize his license.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did the owners --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Oh, pardon me. Did the owner say he was unwelcomed there?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir. The --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did he say he was doing --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: He said that he had no objection to his being there.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: But he did say further --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did he say he was doing anything objectionable to him?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: He did not say that he was doing anything objectionable to him in the record. What he did say was that he would object to dancing, if there were dancing because of course, that would forfeit his license.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: If there were dancing.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Or words to that effect, that's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And he saw it, then he said there was nothing objectionable to him.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did he say -- did he say that this man did not buy anything or did he say that he didn't serve him anything?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: He said this, “When the -- the police testified that when he came in, that he had neither bought anything to -- neither anything to eat nor drink.”

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did he say --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now, on direct examination --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did he say -- did he say positively that he did not buy anything to eat or drink?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: At the trial, he said -- he -- this is what he said positively.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Where (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: He said that he did not know whether he had bought anything to eat or drink.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Isn't that different?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: And he could've -- that he could've bought something.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Isn't that different than to say that he did not buy anything, as you have just stated?He said he didn't know of him having buy -- bought anything and he had other people in there who were serving customers, did he not?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Then, why do you tell us that he said that he did not buy anything?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: What -- well, Your Honor, if I may tell you what I said --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, it's going to --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I said, when the police officer --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) show me -- show me in the record where -- what he did say.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: On -- now, this is at the time of the officer's testimony --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I don't care when it is. Just show me in the record where he said the man positively did not buy anything. That's all I want to know.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: On page 2, Your Honor, testimony of Officer William Lacefield.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, Officer Lacefield couldn't testify that he didn't buy anything. He only came in there at the moment he arrest him. This man had been in there for half hour. You were talking about the manager of the -- of the inn.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Your Honor --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Where did he say the --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Your --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Where did he say?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: All right. Now, let me make this point. Your Honor, I'm telling you what Officer Lacefield said at the time when he testified as to the arrest. On this question, he said, “I walked over and asked the bartender if he had bought anything in there and how long he had been there and he told us that he had been there a little over a half hour and that he had not bought anything.” All right, now, that's what --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, that's hearsay --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now, that's --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That's hearsay, isn't it? You --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You had the --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That is hearsay.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: The manager himself testified --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- did he not?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now, on the testimony of William Marks (ph) at the trial at -- that's on page 28, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, I have it.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: You do know this question, “You do know this fellow, Thompson, had been there for half an hour and had not eaten anything?” Answer, “I didn't see him eat anything.” Question, “If he had had anything to eat or to drink, you would have known about it.” Answer, “It seems like I would.” Question, “Because you were there all the time?” Answer, “Yes.” Now later on, what Your Honor is referring to, at the bottom of the page, “But you never saw him buy anything.” Answer, “No. That don't mean he didn't buy anything. He was standing out there on the floor.” That is the part to which Your Honor is referring to.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: He didn't say that he never bought anything. He just said that he didn't see him buy anything.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, that is -- that is true, but --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And he said he could have bought something without his seeing.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes, all right.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: That's all I wanted to know.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: The point that I was making was that at the time of the arrest --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: The point that I was making that at the time of the arrest, according to Lacefield, the arresting officer, he was informed by the manager that he had not bought anything to eat or drink. That was the point I was trying to make to Your Honor. And if I didn't do that, I apologize to the Court.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Frick, may I ask you --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I ask you -- may I ask you this question. Assume that one takes your view of the evidence just as you present it, as you stated. What I would like to have your views on this is whether, under Kentucky law -- Louisville ordinance law, finding a man in the saloon who shuffles, one doesn't know whether he's done it out of exuberance for 2 minutes or for 20 minutes and assume further that he hasn't bought any drink or a sandwich, does that constitute loitering or vagrancy under Louisville ordinance law?A nd if it does, if that's all the evidence established there, is that consonant with the Fourteenth Amendment? The two questions I'd like you to answer.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, I think, briefly and this particular case has demonstrated. It was so held to constitute loitering under the ordinance. The ordinance provided --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is finding a man in a saloon who happens to shuffle his feet with -- and hasn't bought a drink yet, that constitutes loitering and vagrancy? And that's what -- and that's the -- if that's the construction of the statute --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- might be the ordinance of the statute, you think that makes -- that doesn't defend the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I think it -- well, of course, Your Honor, this is an ordinance rather than a statute --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, well --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Ordinance of the city --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- for all purposes --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I believe that -- that the facts is --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's all there is. If that's all --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's all -- that's all there is as far as the loitering charge. As far as the disorderly conduct charge is concerned, that was another piece of evidence (Voice Overlap) which I'd like to tell the Court.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Mr. Frick, may I declare what you just answered to Mr. Justice Frankfurter, as you said that no basis for the conviction for disorderly conduct is relied upon as having happened in the bar, is that true?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And it's only the loitering conviction that depends on what happened in the bar.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Are you're going to go to the disorderly conduct charge as based on what happened on the sidewalk, is that it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: The disorderly conduct evidence --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But before you do that, would you answer whether it's constitutionally allowable for the City of Louisville to make it an offense to be in a barroom and to do nothing else than to shuffle, whatever that may mean or you've said a bit and not have bought a drink or in the barroom, does that -- that's made a crime.Is that allowable under the Constitution of --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I believe, Your Honor, that it is -- with this background, that loitering ordinances and statutes are recognized to be an -- constitutional exercise of power.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, you can't say --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It can't be practically as broad as that.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You cant -- are you suggesting that merely before the legislative body, whether of the state or a city, says something in to be loitering, that's an end of the matter and you he can't inquire into it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir. I don't say that at all, Your Honor, but I'm just giving that background.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now, the next point --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: The background you give I can't accept, namely, that all loitering statutes and all vagrancy statutes are constitutional. I should think there's a great deal to be --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- said to the contrary.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, I think Your Honor is quite right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So this Court has so held in us in the servitude.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Suppose he --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now, the question is --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Suppose he was in a department store and was shuffling alone in a department store and haven't purchased anything for half hour. Would he be guilty of loitering?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I think, under certain circumstances, he might. It would depend on the particular facts in this case, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: There'd be a lot of women in jail, sir.[Laughter]

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, that's true, but now, you make this distinction. If those women are in there for the business purpose of the store, namely, to shop or to -- or to apprise themselves the prices for the object of future purposes, that would be a different situation. But where the person would go into a store with not the single desire or purpose ever to indulge in business with that proprietor of those premises --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Ever as long as they live?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Ever as long as they live or at any time at all --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Do you say that --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I think that under those circumstances, that could constitute loitering.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Do you say that this evidence shows that -- that this man, no matter how long he might live, had no intention ever of buying anything in this store because of the evidence you have here?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I don't think it does.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, of course --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: But --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: How does that apply then to the department store?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, what I'm saying is this. That it's a concept that -- that a business operator invites the public for his business purpose. Now, the business purpose of this barroom was to sell food and to sell drink. The business purpose is not to provide a place to wait for a bus, nor is it, quite clearly, a place where one may dance.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Isn't the purpose of a place -- of a place of business what the operator of it considers it to be? And if he considers a man was properly there, if he wasn't doing anything that was specifically unlawful, isn't he there lawfully?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: But he is there upon the expressed permission of the proprietor or by -- on the implied permission, I think he is there properly.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: If he's there under the expressed or implied, you say?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well,

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- didn't -- didn't the owner say that the man was welcome there that he had no objections to him being there and haven't done anything offensive? What more could you -- what more could you ask to have -- have an implied willingness for him to be there?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I have expressed my opinion, namely, that the implied indication was only for the purpose of the business. Now, the fact that there are a crowd of people there and there was no objection by the proprietor or the operator of the premises, I think is evidence which should be considered.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: But --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- Frick, I don't know about Louisville, but in -- but in New York and Boston, I suppose thousands and thousands of people enter saloons on errands of necessity.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I suppose that's quite true, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And don't buy a drink, I mean.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: To -- to get back to the evidence, there's very little evidence of disorderly conduct. It's contained in the record that, when he is -- that when Sam Thompson was taken outside the barroom, he argued back and forth with the policeman and thereupon, they placed the disorderly conduct charge against him. That -- that is -- that is the evidence which relates to the disorderly conduct.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) that's all you claim that constitute disorderly conduct?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: You do not relate the shuffling inside disorderly conduct?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: On one thing --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- what as he got outside, it's in the record inside taken outright while he waited for what you call in the  right?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I presume so.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, that's what the record shows to tell me. There were some arguments between the officers.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now, a man protested that he was being persecuted because he's been arrested several times before for minor offenses and there was an argument of it and that's if --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, to the extent of it, I don't know. The testimony is very sketchy on that part. It was just that he argued back and forth with the arresting officer and on that basis --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Not disorderly conduct under your ordinance?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, to get back to these offenses again, I don't think it's the greatest evidence in the world, but I think it is sufficient evidence of disorderly conduct.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: To put a --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: In other words --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: -- man in jail.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- to create a disturbance on the streets.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: To put a man in jail -- you could put a man in jail for -- for arguing back and forth with the police officer when he's put under arrest, is that correct?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's what happened in this case, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And you say that's correct? You should -- is competent evidence and sufficient evidence to send a man to jail?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I think that evidence of argument which results in public disorder is sufficient evidence.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Was there anything in the -- in the evidence to say that it resulted in public disorder?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, then why do you add that to it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I don't know how to answer that question. I think that the first thing to do would be just to read this part of the record which relates to it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Well, there -- there isn't -- I don't think anybody is arguing with you that all the evidence shows is that he argued back and forth with the officers about his arrest. Now --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That is correct. I --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And that --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I will -- I will say that that's what the evidence shows, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: And not -- not as all that shows --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- in connection with the disorderly conduct.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And you say that that's sufficient under you laws, to put a man in jail?

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That's what your court knows.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: To arrest him for disorderly conduct.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And to convict him. That's exactly what this case is about.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Exactly, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Mr. Frick, may I ask -- ask -- mind what -- join with me at the top of page 3.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, those three lines, “Went outside, he was very argumentative. He argued with us back and forth and so then, we placed a disorderly conduct charge on him.” Is that the whole basis of this conviction?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Of disorderly conduct, yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Just those three lines?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Those three lines, yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, there's nothing then to indicate his demeanor when he was arguing?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Do we know whether it was boisterous or whether it was quiet?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: We know no more than what is contained in those three lines, from one line at the bottom of page 2 and the three lines at the top of page 3.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: So that, he might have been discussing his arrest very quietly with the officers.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, if -- if that is compatible with the idea of argument.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, isn't it, or is it? Do you think it's not?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I -- I do not know whether that is compatible. It seems to me that the idea of argumentativeness might be loud and -- and normally --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, this is an argument that's going on here.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) but there is -- there's -- there's nothing in the record to indicate it was loud argument or quiet argument or any other kind (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, you're making an argument now, aren't you?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I hope so, Your Honor. [Laughter]

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Is -- isn't this testimony compatible with his having made the same kind of argument with the police officer?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I suppose so, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: But he said in himself on page 24 with respect to the contrary.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir. I believe his testimony was to that effect.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Is that where you were --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I don't remember.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- were you -- “Were you or not respectful to the officers?” Answer, “Yes, I was. I've never been any other way. I just asked them what they are arrested me for.” Question, “You didn't argue with them? You just asked them what they were arresting you for?” “Yes, I asked what he was locking me up for and he said for loitering and vag” was that, vagrancy?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's vagrancy, yes, sir, Your Honor. That's a colloquial or abbreviation of the crime of vagrancy.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: You say that is a disputed of issue of fact between whatever it meant by the officer's testimony and --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- you were arguing and this man's testimony that he was just an --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: There was no argument.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- impolite inquiry. You say that's an issue of fact.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That there was no argument.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: What do you say, sir, about the two questions involved, number one, is there any forum in Kentucky where this man might, under these circumstances, seek relief from the judgment of the $10 fine imposed by the police court would have --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir. I believe that there could have been. Under Section 110 of our Constitution which is the one that gives to our Court of Appeals jurisdiction over inferior courts. Now, as mentioned by my opponent, Mr. Lusky, the Kentucky cases are to the effect that the mere monetary amount is insufficient to confer jurisdiction under Section 110 on our Court of Appeals, because that would amount to a substitute for an appeal. But all of these cases, like Fowler and also in this particular case here, say that where there is a great and irreparable injury that then, an application may be made to Section 110 of the Kentucky Constitution.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But, in this case --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: In this case, could an appeal have been taken from this conviction?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: To a higher court --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- an appeal could not have been taken.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- to a higher court in Kentucky?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: An appeal as such could not have been taken. I believe that an application could have been made to the Kentucky Court of Appeals under Section 110, the right to control an -- inferior courts setting forth this alleged gigantic conspiracy between the Louisville police judge and the Louisville police force.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And is that --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: And that would have been such a great mere reparable injury that under those circumstances, the Kentucky Court of Appeals would have taken jurisdiction. Now, let me point out one more thing --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: May I -- may I shorten, I read this discussion by suggesting that either an appeal could have been taken to a higher court in Kentucky or it couldn't have in whatever the form in which appeals are taken in Kentucky about which I know nothing. If an appeal is -- could be taken to a higher court, then you should oppose the jurisdiction of this Court because if an appeal could be taken to the highest court, we haven't got jurisdiction of an inferior court. Now, which is it, could an appeal be taken to a higher court (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I believe that it could have, under the facts in this case, under Section 110 --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you are (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- on the constitution.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: When you are to argue against our jurisdiction because you rightly haven't got jurisdiction.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, shall I take one minute for that, Your Honor?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I don't know if (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Not to please --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Here's the situation.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Not to please me, but if the law warrants you.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Here's a situation. In this particular case, there was a writ of habeas corpus applied for to our Circuit Court to stay the execution of the sentence of this police court pending its review by this Court. It was issued by the Circuit Court. The City of Louisville objected on the ground that there is no such jurisdiction under our law. It was appealed to the Court of Appeals. Now, this is a collateral proceeding on habeas corpus and on this collateral proceeding, the Court of Appeals reversed the circuit judge on a habeas question -- habeas corpus question, saying that it alone had jurisdiction under Section 110 and said further, recognizing this fact that in a $5 or $10 appeal as such, there was no review and therefore, the only remedy was to the Supreme Court of the United States. They say further, they -- by dicta, they set out this other idea that if there is a great and irreparable injury, this Court will take jurisdiction. They also recite that this was an extreme case. Now, I believe that -- without speculating, what a court would have done. That in this collateral proceeding, if they felt that that was sufficient under Section 110 to take jurisdiction and on their own motion, order a stay of proceedings in the Louisville Police Court so that they might go to this Court. That that court likewise, if they consider this case as important, would have the authority when the principal case and on the merits, comes before to taking grant relief. Now, that is my position, but the Court of Appeals was never given an opportunity --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is that -- is that argued in your brief, Mr. Frick?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- as such --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is that argued in your brief?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: It's argued some places in my brief.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Where?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I don't remember exactly where. I'd like to make just one more point. Now, as far as the Kentucky Court of Appeals is concerned, this is direct from the Louisville Police Court to the Supreme Court of the United States. The Court of Appeals of Kentucky has not been asked to grant relief to this petitioner on any violations of constitutional rights. Now, he has constitutional rights both under the Federal Constitution and under our State Constitution. They had never been asked to do that, so, I don't see how that you can complain about the Kentucky Court of Appeals when they have not had an opportunity to pass on the merits of this question, but only on the collateral point which they considered important enough to grant relief on their own motion.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: I wonder if I correctly understood you or you -- if I did, you really said what you meant. I understood you to say that the Court of Appeals, on this state proceeding, said that the only place where the man could have relief was here.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Did you say that?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That -- that is correct.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well then, that's to say they couldn't grant any, is it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I don't know what you can say unless the question is presented to a court. Bear in mind, this is a collateral proceeding. Under Section 110, the Kentucky Court of Appeals grants relief on its own motion. They grant a stay of proceedings without being asked for to the petitioner. Now, if they have that authority and deem it important enough in a collateral manner, they certainly have authority in the principal matter to consider it important enough.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: I didn't understand from anything in your brief (Inaudible) again that you claimed that we didn't have jurisdiction.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I believe I raised it.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Where is it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I didn't make any specific claim as such.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: I didn't see it.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I was talking about the right of -- right of review.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, whether you claim it or not, if there be opportunity of reviewing this police court judgment in a Kentucky court, then we haven't got jurisdiction whether you have brought it home to our understanding or not. Either there is or isn't, reviewing power in -- from higher court in Kentucky of this police court judgment, if there is, then we have directed jurisdiction.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Is there --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- the only way that I can ask, it may -- we may or may not have jurisdiction in the Kentucky Court of Appeals. I'd like for the Court to understand that, that it is discretionary under Section 110, whether or not, the Kentucky Court of Appeals will review. Now, they have said that in a case where there is a simple fine of $5 or $10, they will not grant relief under Section 110. But, where a great and irreparable injury is done, then they will grant relief under Section 110. So, Mr. Frankfurter -- Judge Frankfurter, I don't know how to answer your question whether there is or is not an appeal.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: It's speculation. That's why I've started off by saying that there was speculation in this case.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: But you are the one who had asked.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: They were not asked. That is correct, Your Honor, as such.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I understood you to say that they granted relief that was not asked at all.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: They did on the collateral point, that is, a stay of proceedings.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: And his relief on what basis?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: On the basis of Section 110, they --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- say that we have a power under Section 110 of the Kentucky Constitution over control of the inferior courts to grant this right.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Is that the one you say -- is that the one you say they could have granted more relief on -- if that --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: -- in the regular question, maybe they could?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Maybe they could. I don't know.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well, why are -- how are we to assume that they would decide, if they didn't get to this is the only court they had to rely if you are now saying that on the same facts presented to them and asked them to grant direct appeal by the judge? How can we accuse the -- if that were for your court? They said, didn't it, that this is the only place you'd get relief.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: They said that in the collateral proceeding. Yes, sir.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But now --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: But now, they were not asked for relief on the other point. Now, there may or may not --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: But they were now being asked for the relief they granted, were they?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Excuse me, sir?

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I understood you to say they were not even asked for the relief they did grant.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's right. On their own -- on the court's own motion that granted the stay of proceeding in the collateral case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You would ask habeas corpus, a writ. You would ask for a writ. There was a -- there was a proceeding before your court indicated by this petitioner.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Initiated by the City of Louisville. We appealed in the collateral.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you (Voice Overlap) what he did.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He started a -- he started habeas corpus, didn't he?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And he's got to the Court of Appeals.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: We appealed. Then --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So, you appealed.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Then, the Court of Appeals in the opinion which is attached to this -- an appendix to the petition for certiorari says that we have the power. We will not require the petitioner to file a new application with us under 110, when we will grant the relief anyhow. So, we will hereby authorize a stay of proceedings, which was not asked for by the petitioner in the appeal.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: I noticed in your -- state constitution says, referring to the power of the Courts of Appeal, said courts shall have power to issue such writs as may be necessary to give it a general control over inferior jurisdiction. That seems to be very broad.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: How has that been construed?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That has been construed for such writs as writs of prohibition to present -- to prevent great and irreparable injury.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Has it been construed to apply to a case where, although there is no direct right of appeal because of the size of the fine, as here, that none (Inaudible) the Court of Appeals under certain situations will issue extraordinary writs to review those cases?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir. There isn't -- there is another case which says that where a constitutional right may be involved, I think it's the Fowler case, where a constitutional right may be involved but the fine is only $5 or $10 and there's no other showing of great or irreparable injury, relief will not be granted. That's the reverse of the situation.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: What's that case?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's -- that's cited by the petitioner in his brief, Fowler versus --

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Fowler?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: What is it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Walters.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Walters against Fowler. But now, in that particular case, there was no showing of this gigantic conspiracy which we have in this case which may make a difference. We're -- we're asked to speculate on what the Court of Appeals would do.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, you suggested another difference, namely, you suggested the difference between a case where a fellow appeals from the fine of $10, as to which there's no appealability and where he objects to the fine on grounds of unconstitutionality.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: This Walters versus Fowler --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Cases -- cases in different states make that distinction between appealability where a constitutional question is involved and where there isn't involved.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Your Honor, in this Walters versus Fowler, I'd like to be fair about this. In Walters versus Fowler, there was a $5 fine, I believe, and a question as to the constitutionality of the imposition of the fine. And the Court of Appeals, under Section 110, refused to take jurisdiction on the ground that this was just a minor fine and there was no showing of great and irreparable injury.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, Mr. -- Mr. Frick, what -- tell me, what do you understand that your court meant in this case by this sentence in the opinion at page 39? It is only in extreme cases, like the one at bar, where a person wants to go to the Supreme Court, I gather, that means this Court --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: -- that we will interfere with an inferior court under Section 110 when an unappealable fine has been imposed?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That was the language which I was mentioning. I don't know exactly what was in the mind of our Court of Appeals, but they say extreme cases and --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: At page 39 of the --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes. Yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: -- appendix of petitioner's brief.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now, this was considered an extreme case on the collateral proceedings, sufficient for them to grant relief.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: Yes, if you say so. The application that you made in accordance directed you notwithstanding the unappealability case otherwise, they wouldn't appeal. Otherwise, for how do you think you've depended on the next supreme case pose a much less sentence.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I don't attempt to forecast what the Court would do. What I am saying is that here is a case -- this very case itself on collateral proceedings where they did entertain jurisdiction under Section 110 voluntarily. Now, if an application will be made on the merits, would the facts in this particular case be different from the facts -- sufficiently different from the facts in Walters versus Fowler, that they would take jurisdiction? That is the question.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Well, this certainly indicates that they have power to entertain it, does it not? It does that much.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: And we can't speculate as to what they would or would not have done without having an application made.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: And therefore, I'd suppose you say that -- no jurisdiction up here.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Well, I believe that can very well be argued, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES WHITTAKER
turns.text: And you don't argue it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I do argue it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Frick, you spoke at the beginning of your argument about some irrelevancies that have crept into the -- the record itself. I noticed in your brief, as Appendix B, you attached the police record of Sam Thompson from January 24th, 1958 to June 24, 1959. Is that taken from the record?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I would like to put it this way.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: No. Let's ask -- -- just -- just answer the question, if you please. Is it in the record or not?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Not as such, it is not in the record.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now, let me tell you why we did that.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Why is it in your brief?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Now, it's in my brief. On -- the record in the trial of this case on February the 3rd, the record shows that on page 31, that the record was shown to the police court. I believe that -- I believe that this Court is entitled to the same evidence which a police court was shown.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did you give us that? As I understood it that, the -- the record said that the police had handed -- handed the judge the record to this man and it showed 54 arrests.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir. That is correct.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Before the --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: It showed 54 arrests.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Did you show the 54 arrests?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir. We showed nine arrests, that is for the period of the one year preceding the arrest.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: The reason for that is this. I -- the other arrests are not material. There is no point in cluttering up the record with that. The arrests in the year preceding are admissible because, under our alcoholic beverage statute, it is illegal for the holder of an alcoholic beverage license to sell liquor to someone who has been convicted three times in the previous year, three times or more of drunkenness in a public place. That's what DPP is. Now --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But that isn't in the record. This isn't in the record.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Not as such. The -- the criminal record of this man of 54 arrests was shown to the police court and the judge, at page 31, said, “Let the record show that the prosecutor showed me this record.” The basis is not important. It's just to show that there was some jeopardy of the license holder if he would want to sell beer to Mr. Thompson who claimed that he bought a bottle of beer on the premises.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Let me ask you, Mr. Frick. Has this man been arrested since this time?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I understand that he has.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: How many times?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: 12 times.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: How many times?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: 12, Mr. Lusky says.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: 12 times for this same kind of offense?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I'm afraid I can't answer this, Your Honor. Mr. Lusky would have to answer that question.

turns.name: LOUIS LUSKY
turns.text: Well, there's a great variety of crime. We could give you a list, if you want.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: What?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I'd be willing to --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: All right, if you don't (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I'd be -- I'd be willing to stipulate that the --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: If you don't know, you can't answer.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: -- list of subsequent arrests may be given to the Court for full information.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Do we have to pass on that, too?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Excuse me, sir?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Do we have to pass on that, too?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I hope not, Your Honor, but I don't have to -- we've got into some other irrelevance either.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: There is lack of jurisdictional question, but I'm looking for the opinion of your Court of Appeals city ordinance, page 38, you're referring to Walters case, against Fowler, isn't it?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: And you say that although if they had jurisdiction -- had jurisdiction in that case, understanding the unappealable fine to grant extraordinary writ, but they did not in that case because (Inaudible) were extreme. And then, they go on and say that the difference between that case and this one is and in that case, the collateral would be to be to stay in the -- in the case as that kind of a thing that would -- seem they will exercise their jurisdiction on a showing of necessity short of what might persuade them to take the case on direct review. Do I read that all correctly?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's my understanding of the law.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Where is that?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: It's on page 38.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I didn't understand -- I didn't understand your answer to Justice Harlan.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: That's -- I said that is my understanding of the law, that the Court of Appeals does have jurisdiction under Section 110 in an extreme case.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Has it ever taken it in a case like this?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: It -- it has never taken one in a case like this.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: You have heard of it taking on all three particulars?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: They have taken -- I do recall of another case where the Court of Appeals did take jurisdiction under Section 110, which was not on a constitutional case, but which was one which was not otherwise authorized by a statute.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Is that cited, that case?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir. That is not cited.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Could you cite it to us?

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: I -- I can find it in -- I suppose, forward to the clerk.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. Lusky --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Could you do that?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- argued that this --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I beg your pardon.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Will -- will you do that?

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Sure, we'll like to have it.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Will you get that case and file it with the clerk and serve it onto counsel, please.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I get your -- Mr. Lusky's argument. He argued that this is an extraordinary case on the hopes it hasn't arisen every day in Kentucky -- in Louisville.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: Unfortunately --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) case, we don't know what they would have done.

turns.name: LOUIS LUSKY
turns.text: Unfortunately, Your Honor, I'm not in the position to argue. This is an extraordinary case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, you're not in a position to argue otherwise, unless there's -- a record has made out such a case -- or your brief has made it out and neither of which is true.

turns.name: LOUIS LUSKY
turns.text: I'm afraid that it is a very ordinary case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, but you haven't shown that. That isn't the case you're making. You made this particular fellow as a -- as an outcast of Louisville society.That's your argument. That was your argument yesterday.

turns.name: LOUIS LUSKY
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't know what this will be now.

turns.name: LOUIS LUSKY
turns.text: That is true, Your Honor. And all I'm saying is that we don't claim that Sam Thompson is the only outcast that has been created by the Louisville Police Court or even that it's a very ordinary thing in Louisville. And we assume that the reason that this Court has been willing to give its time to this case is that it's not an ordinary thing. Now --

turns.name: JOHN HARLAN, II
turns.text: Do you deny that it had the power unless --

turns.name: LOUIS LUSKY
turns.text: I would like to speak of this jurisdictional question because, as Mr. Justice Frankfurter says, it is a basic question and -- and it goes to the -- to the root of this Court's power to hear the case. I -- I would like to ask one thing. I -- I didn't understand whether Mr. Frick said that this case which he intends to send to us is a case where the Court of Appeals entertained an application for relief, although the sentence was too small for appeal. And I would like to ask whether it is such a case.

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: No, sir. That is not what I -- the case that I had in mind. The case that I had in mind was one, if my recollection is correct, where there was a provision for the incorporation of a city where there was no right of appeal and a constitutional question was raised and the Court of Appeals granted an appeal either directly or under (Inaudible) or go with saying on a constitutional point, that always (Inaudible) That is the case, it's Miller --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: You may --

turns.name: HERMAN E. FRICK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) versus Miller.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes. You may make the record of it and give it to the Court, please.

turns.name: LOUIS LUSKY
turns.text: If the Court please, I -- I am going to do my best to confine myself the 10 minutes that's been allowed. I -- I have taken note that some indulgence had been granted to opposing counsel. And if I run over a little bit, I -- I hope I may be forgive, particularly since this basic question of jurisdiction has been raised. Now, I think it's quite important to understand exactly what the Court of Appeals said. This Court has a pretty clear conception of it now, as these questions have indicated. The situation in the Kentucky Court of Appeals is that -- that substantive relief will not be granted under the power established by Section 110 of the constitution, even though a federal constitutional claim, a due process claim is made, if the sentence is too small for appeal. This appears in a series of cases which is cited in our brief, (Inaudible) is the latest I think, Walters against Fowler, Thompson against Wood .And Walters against Fowler, which is the case where the point is most fully discussed, as it happens, does not specifically refer in the Court's opinion to Section 110. So, you can't tell from reading the Court's opinion that it's a Section 110 case, although it is the only source of jurisdiction. Now, in order to -- I thought this might puzzle the Court when the time came to read the opinion and in -- in the Walters case. And therefore, in order to eliminate any doubt on that score, I have obtained from the clerk of the Kentucky Court of Appeals, under the seal of that court, a certified copy of the petition for relief in Walters against Fowler, which shows that relief was sought under Section 110 of the constitution. This is a little technical question that could come up and if there is no objection, I'd like to file it in the case.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: No objection.

turns.name: LOUIS LUSKY
turns.text: The -- now, here is exactly the -- what the Court of Appeals said, we had -- for the reasons which I explained yesterday. We were under the necessity of keeping the petitioner out of jail for -- until we could get certiorari ruled on in this Court so that the case wouldn't become moot. We thought that the proper -- proper remedy - remedy was habeas corpus in Circuit Court, which does have power to fix bail where it's required by law and we -- and the circuit judge agreed with us and granted it. The city took an appeal from that to the Court of Appeals.
